title: 1.1 Changing our POV
content:
Changing Your POV# “Unless you make the subconscious conscious, it will direct your life and you will call it fate.”
— Carl Jung
TLDR# Shifting your POV — what Justin calls paradigm shifting, or PS — is very difficult, but it is probably the most effective way to change yourself, and maybe others.
Our paradigms shape our attitude and behaviors, which shape our actions.
One common application of this is if you see everybody in your class as an opponent, you simply won’t see opportunities that could benefit everybody — especially when that would actually put you in a better spot than your solo grinding!
More on that later in the chapter “abundance > scarcity.”
PS: Very often, the problem isn’t out there, but rather how you’re seeing it.
We often like to think that what we see is what is, but in reality, it’s what appears to us. We can choose to use the right lens — that is, the right principles — that will let us see what is.
START# You’ve probably experienced an “Aha!” moment in your life. We are going to talk about those moments.
More formally, we think an “Aha!” moment really is just a paradigm shift.
What Does Shifting Your Paradigm Mean?# It means changing the way we interpret our surroundings.
One way to see it is changing your frame of reference: from one frame of reference, an orbit can be a perfect circle, while from another, the same one is spaghetti. It just depends on whose POV you’re looking through — through the broader view, or through the little poor critter that’s getting dragged around the orbit?
Again, you’ve probably experienced one already, like when doing a hard math problem or when reading a great book.
A paradigm shift, or PS, is not a change in your attitude or your personality. Those are secondary pieces.
We’re talking about a change in your character, deeper than any “technique.” That’s a primary, more fundamental piece.
We see character as how you interpret and see things. Outside stimulus must first be interpreted by your paradigms/maps before you can use your attitude/personality to react to it.
To interpret the world with a good POV is to adopt the POV of natural laws, or principles.
I also see these principles as really powerful tools that have magic in them!
According to Stephen Covey, these principles are universally used to build all nations and cultures; it’s just that they interpret them differently. For example, there is a universal sense of fairness, kindness, etc.
It’s up to us to use them or not.
Because of their natural status, they will always govern our lives regardless of whether we use them or not.
Well, we suggest that you use them.
Note from Nathan# POV shifts are good because they’ll help you get along with others a lot better.
It makes you more empathetic, more pleasant to get along with, and also helps you regulate your emotions and not hurt people needlessly when somebody does something you’d consider wrong.
Everything somebody does makes sense to themselves. Your job is to find out why.
Example of Neglecting a Principle# Take a principle like:
“You reap what you sow.”
Let’s say all my teachers have taught me that I can only cram to win: I must only use shortcuts.
Then when I have the opportunity to make friends, I’m only going to use “little tricks” to make them “instantly” like me, by faking interest and glazing them.
Then over time, when they get to know me, they see through my fake persona and I’ll be rightly exposed as a fraud!
“Only when your map is right, THEN attitude and behavior can work wonders.
[So] we must look at the lens through which we see the world, as well as at the world we see.”
— Stephen Covey
Here’s an example showing why:
Imagine you have just arrived in SF for the first time and need to find Lowell High School.
The catch is you have a map of New York!
Well, it’s easy to predict that no matter how hard you try, you won’t find Lowell High School.
Ok, maybe after an egregious amount of time.
If we don’t change our map, the attitude and behaviors we have don’t really matter.
“Nah, I’m going to run faster!”
You’re just going to run to the wrong place faster.
“Nah, I’m going to replace my laziness with patience and positive thoughts!”
Ok, let’s face it: by the 5th hour, you’re going to be sweating buckets and disappointed in yourself.
Instead of hacking at the leaves of the problem — thinking that we are just lazy, etc. — we can cut the roots of this problem by getting a new map.
The moral of this story is that you need the right map if you want to reach a destination, and that our paradigms are quite literally the maps we use to navigate our world.
You see, the bad news is we naturally believe that the maps we hold to navigate life are showing us the territory.
The good news is we can get a new map.
When we have the right map, then diligence matters.
Then being patient and being optimistic will pay off in the long run.
If you have the wrong map, you won’t reach your destination.
Paradigms Before Techniques# But we’re not suggesting that those secondary pieces like communication skills or being optimistic aren’t beneficial.
Covey sums this paradigm well:
“Perhaps, in utilizing our human capacity to build on the foundations of generations before us, we have inadvertently become so focused on our own building that we have forgotten the foundation that holds it up; or in reaping for so long where we have not sown, perhaps we have forgotten the need to sow.”
Working on attitude and behavior without first considering your paradigm is like trying to get by your hard classes through cheating/cramming.
You sometimes get by, even with good grades — Mr. Cooley class lmao — but if you don’t pay the price daily, you’ll never achieve true mastery and actually change your mind.
When we change our paradigm to see that there aren’t any shortcuts, then it’ll become obvious to us that studying really is the best way forward.
Studying smart is another thing — that’s in Chapter 5, if you want to join in that conversation!
If you only see studying as slogging through material, then it’s hard to stay diligent over time.
But when you see learning new material as fascinating, then it becomes a lot easier to be locked in, and thus get better results!
A Big Paradigm Shift from a Dying Bishop# When I was young and free and my imagination had no limits, I dreamed of changing the world.
As I grew older and wiser, I discovered the world would not change, so I shortened my sights somewhat and decided to change only my country.
But it, too, seemed immovable.
As I grew into my twilight years, in one last desperate attempt, I settled for changing only my family, those closest to me.
But alas, they would have none of it.
And now as I lie on my deathbed, I suddenly realize:
If I had only changed myself first, then by example I would have changed my family.
From their inspiration and encouragement, I would then have been able to better my country.
And, who knows, I may have even changed the world.
And the most effective way to change yourself is to change your paradigm.
Grab a New Map# By changing our paradigms to reflect reality, we can do things that actually work!
I, Justin, ask myself a lot:
How accurate is my map?
A word of warning though: due to our tendency to treat our maps as realities, changing them will hurt!
This is the same when we try to change other people’s perceptions by convincing them.
Even though we/you are right, it hurts for our realities to get destroyed, despite it being worth it in the long term.
If something bothers you, see if you’re looking at the wrong place.
Try changing the way you see the issue.
We like to treat our maps as realities, but more often than we’d like, they aren’t.
Grab a new map!
A natural question now would be:
“Now HOW do we get new maps?”
That’s the next chapter.

title: 4.1 Bingeing? Scrolling Too Much?
section: 4. Procrastinating? Learn to put the first things first.
content:
4.1 Bingeing? Scrolling Too Much? Solutions!# “In the throes of desire, there’s no deciding. Binding ourselves is a way to be free from temptation.”
— Anna Lembke
TLDR# If you want to stop bingeing, do not just fight cravings in the moment.
Instead, understand the pleasure-pain cycle, accept some boredom, and build systems that protect you before desire takes over.
The Problem# “Oh I… just scrolled five hours of YouTube! AHH… I feel like a loser! Like bro, it’s not even fun after the third hour, and yet I still do this!”
We feel you.
We have done the same.
So how should we solve this problem?
A bogus solution is:
“Oh, I’m just lazy and I’m just a loser.”
Nah.
You are not powerless.
You have the ability to change this.
Here are three keys that can help.
1. Understand Why We Binge or Doom-Scroll# Tech is designed to be addictive.
Platforms take advantage of our post-usage cravings.
There are papers and dedicated scientists locking in to make already addictive platforms like YouTube even more addictive.
They are trying to get you to watch “just one more video.”
They are trying to recommend content that is designed to be consumed, not necessarily content that is designed to help you.
Remember their goal:
They want your attention.
Your attention equals their salaries.
Their salaries equal their calories and dinner next winter.
When I play League of Legends, one game often turns into too many games.
Even after years of playing, pleasure never really feels like “enough.”
Isn’t that some toxic design, bro?
I hate you, Riot.
No, I love you, Riot.
You have given me an opportunity to help people.
2. Boredom and Loneliness Make the Internet More Appealing# Modern technology is built to be addictive.
But boredom and loneliness make it even more tempting.
Elisa put it well:
“I truly believe the internet is so addicting because we are bored and lonely in real life. You need to have a very fun life for the internet to not look fun.”
Social proof also makes excessive doom-scrolling seem normal.
After all, we humans are social creatures.
More often than we would like to admit, we follow the crowd.
That is why it is important to stick with people who lift you up.
Tough clubs, teams, and communities can build resilience.
3. Feeling Bad After Bingeing Means You Are Human# If you feel horrible after wasting a few hours, or even a whole day, congrats.
You are human.
We often feel horrible after too much pleasure because of homeostasis.
Specifically, the pleasure-pain balance can help us understand what is happening.
Maybe you learned homeostasis in freshman biology class.
That was one of the only things I remembered, and it is one of the most important parts of this solution.
Thanks, Ms. Killpack.
Homeostasis: Your Body Wants Balance# Homeostasis is your body trying to restore balance.
For example, when you get hot, your body tries to cool down by sweating.
When you get cold, your body shivers to warm back up.
When you exercise like a demon, your body replies by sweating buckets.
When you take a cold shower, your body may feel super warm afterward.
This is the important part:
Just like your body balances hot and cold, it also balances pleasure and pain.
If you press hard on the pleasure side by bingeing five hours of YouTube or gaming all night, your body may reply by making you feel miserable.
If you press on the pain side through something like working out, your body may reply by making you feel great afterward.
If you are in physics, you can think of it like comparing kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy when throwing a ball upward.
Repeated Bingeing Makes the Cycle Worse# Our situation gets dangerous when we scroll and binge repeatedly.
As we fill ourselves with more dopamine, we crave more and feel even worse when our body replies with more pain.
Repetition can lead to tolerance and worse rebounds.
So we end up needing more of our drug of choice to feel the same effect.
So if we know scrolling is bad for us, how can we use this knowledge to solve the problem?
The Willpower Trap# A bogus solution is:
“Oh, I just need a little bit more discipline. Then I’ll shrug off that addictive stuff.”
That is a lie.
How often does that actually work?
For me, I hardly lasted a day.
Later on, I could not even last one sitting.
Trying to muscle your way through with willpower often fails for two reasons:
Our habits overpower momentary intentions.
Cravings mess with our decision-making.
This is especially true if you are trying to forcibly “lock in” over and over again.
In the throes of desire, there is no deciding.
The DOPAMINE Framework# Here is what works better.
The acronym DOPAMINE is the outline of our solution.
This is inspired by Dopamine Nation and Digital Minimalism.
D — Data# Ask yourself:
What are you using, how much are you using it, and how often?
You need to know what is actually happening before you can fix it.
Do not guess.
Track it.
O — Objectives# Ask yourself:
What are your objectives for using this thing?
Even seemingly “irrational” behavior is usually rooted in some personal logic.
Personally, this step was the most important one for me when I was trying to get out of my YouTube addiction.
Here is what I asked myself:
“What deep value is this piece of technology serving?”
For me, I scrolled YouTube because I wanted to be smart.
Being smart was something I valued deeply.
Then came the best question:
“Is this the best way? Do the benefits outweigh the costs?”
Clearly, no.
I was getting great knowledge from wonderful people in the first hour.
But then I would go watch cat videos for the next five hours.
At that point, the costs outweighed the benefits.
This overlaps with the next step.
P — Problems# Ask yourself:
What problems are related to your usage?
Most of us cannot see the full extent of the consequences while we are using.
That is part of the trap.
You need to step back and look at the cost clearly.
A — Abstinence# Abstinence can reveal hidden problems.
Try not using the thing for a month.
It is intense, but it can help a lot.
It also helps to think about your future self.
Ask yourself:
“Do I still want to be bingeing ten years from now?”
Probably not.
M — Mindfulness# Mindfulness means observing what your brain is doing while it is doing it.
But do it without judgment.
That means no:
“Ewww! Why would I be thinking about that? I’m a loser. I’m a freak.”
You are supposed to feel bad when you stop.
Your body is restoring its pleasure-pain balance.
That does not mean you are broken.
It means your body is adjusting.
I — Insight# Insights are the realizations you get when you abstain.
These insights are often not possible while you continue using your drug of choice.
Sometimes you need distance before you can see clearly.
N — Next Steps# Ask yourself whether you want to continue using, stop completely, or use less.
Most people either stop or reduce their usage.
The point is to make a conscious decision instead of staying on autopilot.
E — Experiment# Use trial and error.
Gradually figure out what works and what does not.
That is where the next section comes in.
Binding Yourself# You can use self-binding tactics to protect yourself before desire takes over.
Self-binding can be:
Physical — physically blocking access.
Chronological — limiting use by time.
Categorical — removing an entire category of temptation.
Many suggestions can fit into multiple categories.
Keeping yourself near temptation and trying to fight it head-on is usually a recipe for disaster.
We have tried it.
It does not work well because willpower is not always on call.
Bind yourself while you still possess the capacity for voluntary choice.
Because in the throes of desire, there is no deciding.
Physical Locks# Physical locks prevent you from using something.
They are simple and effective, though they may hurt a bit.
Suggestions# Lock your phone away.
Give it to someone else.
Personally, I love giving my phone to my study partner.
That way, I can still be available for emergencies, but I cannot compulsively check my phone.
I have also heard great things about kSafe kitchen safes, although I have not personally tried them.
A kSafe literally locks itself, and you can only access what is inside when the timer runs out.
Warning# Sometimes the barrier itself becomes a challenge.
Then getting your drug of choice becomes part of the appeal.
So make sure the barrier is actually secure.
Do not just throw your phone across the room and then pick it back up five minutes later to scroll.
Locking by Time# This means you can use the thing only when the clock runs down or when you cross a self-designated finish line.
This helps because high-dopamine goods, like our phones, mess with our ability to delay gratification.
We tend to overvalue the short term and undervalue the long term.
So do not rely on short-term decision-making when you are trying to break a bad habit like watching too much YouTube.
When we give ourselves time, we can evaluate our decisions better.
Suggestions# Work by a timer with friends.
If they catch you lacking, then you get embarrassed.
Hopefully, that helps you focus when you need to lock in.
The kSafe idea can also work here.
Categorical Binding# Categorical binding means preventing entire categories of dopamine sources, not just one specific part.
This can also block possible triggers.
Suggestion# Use the question from earlier:
“Is this the best way? Do the benefits outweigh the costs?”
For example, I may get great knowledge from wonderful people in the first hour of YouTube.
But then I might go watch cat videos for the next five hours.
So for me, I may need to ban all of YouTube, because I do not want to deal with those problems.
Final Cautions# Do not replace one addiction with another.
I switched from YouTube to playing games.
That was not an effective strategy.
Do not swing to harsh extremes.
Take small steps.
Motivated by self-hatred, I once went all-in on being bored.
After one month, I swung back to bed-rotting and room-rotting because I could not stand it anymore.
Seek balance, not self-punishment.
Remember the homeostasis idea.
If you go too hard on yourself, you may revert.
Self-binding helps, but it is not magic.
It has limitations.
Final Reminder# If you want to stop bingeing YouTube, do not just fight cravings in the moment.
Understand the pleasure-pain cycle.
Accept some boredom.
Build systems that protect you before desire takes over.
