title: 1. WELCOME
content:
WELCOME!# “Namaste.”
TLDR# We are high school seniors.
This book attempts to distill all the lessons we’ve learned in our years at high school. The material here embodies “what we wish someone had told us before we started high school.”
Why do we think you should read this? We think pointing out and solving some common pitfalls that high schoolers run into would prove useful in living the best high school life.
START# Here’s what we’re imagining: you’re sitting in a chair, maybe during the registry or lunch, and you’re thinking:
“Lol. Are they just doing it for the college app?”
No, we aren’t. There are a lot more “college efficient” activities than sitting down to write a book — tbh I think this is probably one of the most inefficient things you can do to get into a school.
meme: they got us in the first half, not gonna lie.
Unless you’re truly exceptional, high school is a struggle: perhaps the classes could be hard, we are lonely, or we can’t seem to achieve our goals because we are addicted to scrolling/manga/YouTube. Or maybe it’s not a struggle yet. But trust us, problems are going to arise because you’re human. It’s like driving through SF’s 19th Avenue and trying not to have the car feel like a roller coaster.
Because there’s a 99.99% chance that you could stumble in the many pitfalls — why would us seniors sit back when we can help our younger peers out?
So we are going to do what’s called a pro gamer move: reach out to our younger peers, point out pitfalls, and give our solutions/thoughts.
You see, we made mistakes, and one way to turn it into something positive is to tell you.
I, Justin, learned that when I could finally stomach my ego to ask my older peers for help, I could learn way more than I could do by myself. Not only that, I realized that many of them, especially those scary seniors, are actually really kind! Some guys, like Tyler, had the aura of a sigma. I swear, you can literally hear the sigma grindset theme when those guys are striding through crowds and halls! AND they really wanted to help out!
We can pick up where others left off! You can pick up the tools we’ve forged over the years, use them to soar even higher than us, and when it’s your turn to go, share your insights for the next generation!
This book is our attempt to distill all the lessons my peers and I have learned in our years at high school.
The material here embodies “what we wish someone had told us before we started high school.”
Our mission: make a lasting and positive difference for our younger peers.
Hey, we would clap our hands with delight if we learned that you have done something new and worthwhile from this book, and tried whatever “antics” we suggested.
Why do we think you should read this? We think pointing out and solving some common pitfalls that high schoolers run into would prove useful in living the best high school life.
Disclaimer# By the way, we aren’t here to tell you that you gotta claim the throne of “The AP King/Queen” by taking the most APs in US History. Or to “achieve” a sleep schedule of an alien — that is, a nonexistent one! We won’t sing hymns on how great you are. You already know you are. This book was designed and written to be as helpful as possible to you.
The book is not a collection of stories of past achievements. Instead, I have tried to organize and generalize the lessons of various experiences to produce a set of guidelines for our younger peers.
If you see anything profound here, assume it is from the experts/teachers we learned it from. If you see anything foolish, assume it is our error.
I know that not every book is perfect, and all of them succeed in some aspects and fail in others. So IF YOU HAVE A QUESTION, ASK!! There’s no way we can read your mind and slip answers on your bed. We don’t have the funds to slip your answers through your chimneys like Santa!
So what’s the best way to have the best high school experience?
We don’t know the golden formula.
But we do have some suggestions.
To that end, we offer this book.

title: 1.2 How to receive advice?
content:
How Do We Get New Maps?# TLDR# Maps, or paradigms, are powerful.
Good information shows us a more accurate map.
We can build better maps by having good connections with people and reading good books, among other ways.
Above all: use what we learn.
We shouldn’t hoard knowledge like we’re in a zombie apocalypse, because thoughts unrealized are wasted.
It’s incredibly difficult to understand something without actually using it, because life is often smarter than our ruminations.
Oh, we’re not ready?
Do we need to know “just a little” more?
Nah.
We’re always going to feel unready.
No matter how much we know, improving always requires us to go out of our comfort zone, which is defined as the place where we aren’t comfortable.
We think this comfort zone idea is natural law, so we can’t cheat around it.
We know that effective change stems from changing our paradigm.
So how do we get new maps?
1. Solo Grinding Our Way# Not recommended.
One way is to solo grind our way, but this is hard because we struggle to recognize our maps’ faults.
They look like reality.
I, Justin, don’t recommend this because I’ve tried to, and I could’ve learned so much more, way faster if I did options 2 and 3 instead.
What ends up happening is a lot of unproven theories and mediocre results.
It’s incredibly difficult to understand something without actually using it, because life is smarter than our ruminations.
Need an introvert person to verify.
But don’t get me wrong: spending time alone is great.
But too much, and we start to delude ourselves.
Don’t be like Junior-year Justin.
2. Making Great Connections# Recommended.
Making great connections is the first great alternative to adopt better paradigms.
Talk to goats → become a goat.
I think this is better than solo grinding because if we meet great people, then they will already make a great influence on us without us needing to conjecture any theories.
A great example — and a big regret of mine — was when my brother was effectively “handed down” the formula by his business professor for finding your way and dealing with distraction.
That was something I spent 3 years building on my own!
I felt jealous, yet happy there’s a better way to make better paradigms.
Yeah, people can really lift you up.
You are the sum of the people you meet, so if you walk with the wise, you become wise.
3. Reading Great Books# Recommended.
Reading great books is the second great alternative, for a very similar reason to making great connections.
We tend to become like the people we admire and hang around.
Books are one way people can “instill” themselves and their thoughts into us.
By reading, you are effectively spending time with the author by taking in their ideas.
Although books are wonderful — they changed my life, and I made a book recommendations list too — they don’t connect you as deeply as actually hanging out with people.
4. Taking Action# Recommended.
Lastly, doing things and taking action.
Capitalize on the wisdom of life, because it is way smarter than whatever you’re ruminating about.
Don’t make decisions.
Make experiments.
Don’t feel finished when you think of something.
Do something, however small it may be.
Now I see life as fairly unfair.
Pitfall: Eternal Student Syndrome# The first thing I, Justin, wished I knew was to not get trapped in a cycle of getting so much advice and not actually acting.
That itself is a downward spiral.
I guess another name for this trap is Eternal Student Syndrome.
This bears repeating:
Avoid the trap of being frozen by learning too much.
Isaac puts it this way:
Don’t let things rot in your mind.
Thoughts unrealized are thoughts wasted.
You need to remember to keep doing 2, 3, and 4, with 1 only in moderation.
We can choose to do this.
If you run into trouble setting up 2, 3, or 4, then you should read Chapter 2 or talk to a goat.
Keep 1 around like a flame: make sure it stays alive, but don’t feed it so much that it burns you down.
Why Do We Fall Into This Trap?# What leads us to this trap?
How can we prevent it from “burning us down?”
One reason we might fall into this pitfall is because we never feel “ready,” or comfortable enough, to improve and actually start making changes.
Over time, we realize that it’s very rare you’ll feel “ready” to use the advice we have here.
If it’s a math concept, then ok, that’s easy to learn its applications: just do a hard problem on that topic.
But what’s more dangerous about this trap?
It snowballs.
The more we freeze ourselves, the harder it is to feel ready, which makes it even more likely that we freeze ourselves deeper.
If we think we are not ready and end up not taking the shot, it’s going to affirm the identity that:
“You aren’t ready.”
Then you’ll look back and say:
“See? I wasn’t ready. Look at all the evidence!”
That is going to kill our motivation and end up making it even less likely to take shots.
And taking shots is one of the best ways to play high school.
The Lesson# No matter how much you know, actually making improvements means moving out of our comfort zones.
And moving out of our comfort zones means, by definition, being uncomfortable.
Look, it’s amazing you’re reading this and not doom-scrolling.
But don’t “doom-read,” or else you’re going to revert, then regret that you didn’t act.
If you feel unready most of the time, you aren’t alone.
Most people will instinctually hesitate and miss opportunities, even though those opportunities could pay off big time.
This permeated my high school years, and even those before us.
Hamlet especially.
It’s hard for our egos to admit, but if your map shows you that hoarding knowledge is better than using the knowledge to achieve results, you are headed for danger.
The Sports Example# Sports make this problem obvious.
If you hesitate too much, the consequence is obvious.
The opponent will move first, and very often, you’ll be at the mercy of your opponent.
Here’s a basketball example.
I only have a few moments to pull up a jumper before the opponent is able to block me.
If I hesitate:
“Oh, what if he’s going to block me?”
then the consequence is obvious:
I don’t get to shoot, and I will have to look for another opportunity.
In high school, the consequences of avoiding uncomfortable growth are not as obvious in the moment.
But they are very obvious when time goes on.
They are obvious to us seniors, so we want to tell you:
DON’T AVOID THE PAINS.
WE REGRETTED IT, AND WE’RE TELLING YOU TO EMBRACE IT.
Because that’s how you’re going to grow.
There are no shortcuts or quick fixes.
The Right Map# We think this is the right map:
The more you obey it, the better your results will be.
Simple as that.
In the world of advice, we depict this phenomenon as:
“Growing involves moving out of your comfort zone.”
There’s a reason why some people improve faster than me.
One of the reasons is that they have accepted that being uncomfortable is part of improvement.
Paradigm Shift# So it’s not:
“Do I want to improve or not?”
Instead, it is:
“To be a little unready, or not to be?”
That is the question.
Thanks, Hamlet.
The feeling of unreadiness is always going to be there, no matter how much you learn or prepare.
We can tell you everything we know, all our shortcomings, and all the lessons we’ve gotten from them.
But if you’re not even moving out of your chair, then what’s the point?
Too much research is just another form of procrastination, which leads, again, to unproven theories and mediocre results.
You won’t understand anything — including whatever words we tell you — unless you actually try it.
When you try something, you will obtain your own interpretation of whatever we say.
And that is 1000% easier to remember than passively reading.
Another Paradigm Shift# Knowledge isn’t power.
The knowledge we implement is power.
We are here to tell you that there isn’t a yellow brick road leading you around.
We carve our own paths here.
We do that by using what we know, making experiments, and adjusting bit by bit every day.
More on that later.
How to Use This Book# Use it.
Act on it.
Experiment with it.
We aren’t your English teachers testing you for how much you can remember.
We are silently scanning around for those who took this to heart.
Uhh, did I get that straight?

title: 2. Stuck overthinking? → Be proactive!
content:
2.0 Agency Problem? Be Proactive# TLDR# “My thoughts be bloody, or be nothing worth!”
— Hamlet
“We can always choose our response.”
— Victor Frankl
Being proactive means focusing on what we can control, accepting what we cannot, and choosing our actions based on our values instead of reacting emotionally to every situation.
Life is too short for pointless fights.
Time spent kicking, yelling, and overthinking things outside our control is time not spent on things we can control.
A proactive person does not ask:
“Why didn’t life give me what I expected?”
They ask:
“It doesn’t matter what I expect of life. What does life expect of me?”
1. What Does It Mean to Be Proactive?# There are parts of life we cannot control.
We cannot change our “spawn point” like Minecraft speedrunners.
But we can control our response.
Being proactive means recognizing your agency in a situation and acting with intention.
It means making choices based on your values instead of reacting to every external impulse, insult, fear, or doubt.
A reactive person says:
“This happened to me, so now I’m helpless.”
A proactive person says:
“This happened. What can I do next?”
You cannot always control how you feel, but you can always decide what to do next.
Either be the jailer of your own cell, or walk through your open prison door.
2. To Be Proactive Means to Focus on What You Can Control# It is easy to get stuck in thoughts like:
“What if I did this differently?”
“What if life was more fair?”
“When will everyone finally recognize that I’m the goat and start glazing me?”
But too much of this becomes fantasy, self-pity, or pointless fighting.
According to my experience, mulling over these thoughts is a waste of time.
A better practice is:
Worry about what you control.
Accept the rest.
Life can take opportunities from us.
Fate can close doors that might have made us better.
But what good is it to live inside imaginary alternate timelines or have conversations with people who don’t exist?
The past is a place to learn from, not a place to live in.
Give quality time for reflection, but not so much that it causes more problems.
3. When We Are Proactive, We Can Choose Our Responses# If someone says:
“I’m better than you at ___.”
We have a choice.
It helps to think of these dialogues like a turn-based game.
Bogus response:
You can slug them.
They deserve it!
Or you can carry that comment around all day:
“AH someone hurt me!”
Or you can simply think:
“Okay.”
Then move on.
This does not mean you become passive.
It means you stop fighting unnecessary wars.
A lot of energy is wasted because we take everything personally.
But if you fight tooth and nail over every little thing, you will be too tired for the things that actually matter.
4. Example: I Didn’t Get into Harvard# Say you worked extremely hard but did not get into Harvard.
You have a choice:
Option 1:
“This is so unfair. I should have gotten in. Everyone else is lucky. Everything I do is somehow wrong and everything they do is right.”
Proceeds to cry.
Option 2:
“This hurts, but life does not care what I expected. What does life expect of me now? I should learn from my mistakes, cheer on others around me, and keep moving forward.”
Doesn’t Option 2 sound way better?
We don’t pretend that disappointment doesn’t hurt.
We just don’t let it spill and make ourselves a mess.
5. Proactive Students Admit Their Ignorance and Ask Questions# This is especially powerful in AP classes, where many people suffer from Imposter Syndrome.
Let’s call that the “AP Imposter Syndrome.”
What does it sound like?
“That person looks like she knows everything.”
“Everyone will think I’m stupid if I ask.”
“I shouldn’t ask my question. It’s dumb.”
Don’t get me wrong.
Yes, people in AP classes are smart.
But aren’t they taking the class because they don’t know everything?
Worrying about other people’s opinions is worrying about things we can’t control yet.
If we are proactive, we can choose to just ask our questions.
If you ask the question, you get more value from class.
The teacher teaches better.
Other students become less afraid to ask their questions too, which will benefit the whole class.
Either way, you are spending the same lifeforce to attend class.
But if you ask questions, you might get 10x the value.
And those smart-looking classmates are probably not thinking about you, but shaking in their chairs from the same problem you’re facing.
Isn’t that worth the shot?
More on this in the next chapter on fear.
6. Don’t Overthink It# Overthinking happens when the more you think, the harder the problem looks.
How do you know when you are overthinking?
A useful boundary I use is:
If I think something over more than three times before doing something, then I tell myself I’m probably overthinking.
Try this out!
At some point, doing the thing gives better information than thinking about it.
Sometimes, we can’t see the benefits until we get our head out of the loop.
7. Dealing with Black/White Thinking# Sometimes we get reactive because our emotions take over.
It looks like:
When getting insulted: “Everyone is out to get me.”
When we mess up: “I’m the world’s greatest loser.”
When we succeed: “I’m the world’s greatest winner. The goat!”
Before we do something we care about: “If this goes badly, everything is over.”
This puts unnecessary stakes on ourselves, and it makes us our own nemesis when we get hurt.
We end up multiplying our own damage on ourselves.
And it’s hard to motivate ourselves when we are bogged down in these problems.
We deal with this not by ignoring our emotions.
Instead, we should:
Recognize this type of thinking. Ask yourself why you feel that way. Journal it if helpful. Talk to trusted friends and adults. These should help you decide your next best move.
8. Being Proactive Is Hard# Being proactive sounds simple, but it is not always easy.
It gets harder when:
You are tired, like coming out of back-to-back tests. You do not know what the next step is. You have bottled-up negative feelings. You distract yourself with devices instead of processing your emotions. So do not expect yourself to always enter “main character mode” through pure willpower.
Here are our thoughts for each obstacle:
Push yourself and judge yourself fairly. Ask others for help. See Chapter 3 for more. Check out Section 7 above. Fix your environment. See Chapters 4 and 5. 9. Some Final Thoughts# Proactivity is not taking everything personally, not overthinking, and choosing our best actions.
Life will throw you garbage that’s outside of your expectations.
When you are there, ask yourself:
“What does life expect of me?”

title: 2.1 Afraid? --> Shift POV and just do something!
content:
Afraid? Shift POV and Just Do Something!# TLDR# The problem: we hesitate when faced with the opportunity to grow.
This fear hurts us because we may end up missing the opportunity, along with the many benefits it could’ve brought us.
So how should we deal with this?
A solution: shift your view.
Use a new map:
What’s true is that you’re excited. Your body can’t tell the difference for some reason. Nobody cares as much as you think. It’s natural to be scared of new things. We always have the choice to ignore this caveman brain’s fight-or-flight reaction. Yeah, our bodies lie to us sometimes.
Don’t be the jailer of your own cell.
What you see or think isn’t always reality, so don’t trap yourself in your own ruminations.
You do not need to feel confident before acting.
Action builds confidence.
“Oh, what if I fail?”
Nah. Just do it and let’s talk after.
“Are you in earnest? seize this very minute—
What you can do, or dream you can, begin it,
Boldness has genius, power, and magic in it!
Only engage, and then the mind grows heated—
Begin it, and the work will be completed!”
— Goethe
Good guideline: if you’ve thought three times before doing something, but “I’m afraid” is the main reason you’re not doing it, that means you should probably just do it.
START# Problem: we hesitate when faced with the opportunity to grow.
This fear hurts us because we end up missing the moment and the many benefits it could’ve brought us.
So how should we deal with this?
A solution: shift your view.
Use a new map:
What’s true is that you’re excited. Your body can’t tell the difference for some reason. Nobody cares as much as you think. It’s natural to be scared of new things. We always have the choice to ignore this caveman brain’s fight-or-flight reaction. Yeah, our bodies lie to us sometimes.
Speaking will come once you get started.
It’s an upward spiral!
Fear Is Like a Cold Toilet Seat# People are just kids, even though we are “seniors.”
I mean that we complain or internally call someone awkward, etc.
I wish I knew to treat fear like sitting on a toilet seat.
The toilet seat is usually very cold and, honestly, a bit uncomfortable for the first five or so seconds before it starts to heat up and feel fine.
But there’s no way I can do something important — in this case, using the bathroom — unless I sit through the few seconds of a cold seat.
Sometimes, you just have to accept that you will almost always feel a bit of fear.
But we also need to accept that we still have the choice to move forward.
It’s not complicated.
Our natural instinct is to make it complicated and end up not doing anything.
But what is a good definition of complicated?
Well, I think thinking something over three times is a good boundary.
If “I’m afraid” is the main reason you’re not doing it, that means you should just do it.
It means you’re facing the toilet scenario from above.
Use your common sense when doing this.
Don’t be reckless, and stay safe.
Please don’t misinterpret this and think:
“Oh, if I cheat, there might be magic!”
Erm, I don’t think so.
Some Reasons Why We Fear# Some reasons for why we fear:
We’re afraid of being “different.” We think we don’t know enough, it’s too hard, or we need more “practice.” We think we might “lose” something. Let’s deal with these one at a time.
1. We’re Afraid of Being “Different”# Most people are not watching you that closely.
They are mostly busy with their own lives and problems.
They won’t really remember that you even attempted something.
So even if you fail, they won’t really care.
We make it as hard as we imagine it to be, because our society is made of humans.
They are probably all thinking the same thing.
Maybe that could reassure us.
2. We Think We Don’t Know Enough# Action helps you gather more information more efficiently than overthinking.
Besides, a completed action beats a perfect action that never happens.
Also, just getting your ideas out can actually help you think better.
For some odd psychological reason, if we just take initiative, then confidence will follow.
I personally barred myself from pursuing opportunities by fooling myself with:
“I’m practicing dude! Just need a little bit more time…”
But according to my experience, usually the opportunity has already passed.
And what hurts more is that I tell myself I’ll get it next time, calm myself down with “all is well,” then proceed to miss another opportunity.
3. We Think We Might “Lose” Something# You think you might lose your “aura”?
Sometimes we may find ourselves valuing our status as being “smart.”
But in reality, our opinions may be wrong and actually don’t matter.
The only way you can lose is by not doing something.
When I went to my school’s math club, I was really scared to ask for help.
I was so self-conscious about how others would treat me differently, and that I wasn’t the “smart-glasses-hoodie dude in the corner” as I appeared to be.
To this day, I am still quite scared to ask for help, even as the math president!
But sometimes, fear will always stare you down.
We just have to stare back at it, know that it’s always going to be there, and just ask.
In my math class, I always get super nervous when I admit my absurd levels of ignorance.
But hey — I’m still alive, and I’ve learned a lot more than if I had not taken any shots.
Growing Means Moving Out of Your Comfort Zone# From Chapter 1.2: seizing the opportunities we are given will always make us uncomfortable.
Growing means moving a little bit out of our comfort zones.
Remember, even if you fail, they won’t care.
And, which is even cooler, they’ll celebrate with you when you succeed!
A nice paradigm shift to deal with fear:
Most people don’t care about what you think or do.
So if you fail, it doesn’t matter because they don’t care.
Why don’t they care?
They have enough problems of their own.
If you do succeed, then people actually celebrate with you.
Kinda crazy, to be honest.
Silence Does Not Make You Cool# Instead, we think we look cool if we are sitting there thinking and doing cool gestures.
Everyone is doing it too.
But in reality, it’s silly.
Lots of us have been doing this for a little more than three years, and we’ve gained close to nothing from being silent.
When I didn’t ask for help during class, there were many times where I had to grind my head off to understand a concept that I could’ve gotten within ten seconds of asking my teacher or classmates for help.
This is also a good study tip:
Allow the teacher to teach you by asking questions.
Remember that no one cares what you are doing, even if you ask a “dumb” question.
And by the way, when you step up, others will follow, and it becomes easier for you to keep going.
As Goethe said, boldness has magic in it.
Some Potential Obstacles# Elisa points out some potential obstacles.
Unfortunately, we suffer the “easier said than done” curse.
Classrooms are not always oriented to encourage conversation.
We fear rejection from trying to talk to someone we find interesting.
When things don’t turn out the way you wanted, stay proactive.
The right friends and mentors make it easier to implement the ideas we’ve discussed.
Dealing with the AP Class Scenario, AKA Imposter Syndrome# Almost everywhere, but especially in AP classes, we think our peers are so smart.
But in reality, they are taking the class because they are not so smart.
Often, people only seem smart to others because they don’t do enough for you to see their mistakes.
Practice:
Just ask a question or answer once.
Take small steps.
Besides, if you’re thinking that so-and-so is going to think you’re awkward, the reality is:
Nobody cares!
We just have to accept that fear always takes a seat on your shoulder to whisper random fantasies in your mind.
We must also accept that it is up to us to move forward and act.
Notice# We aren’t trying to eliminate fear.
We just want to place our fear in the right places.
There’s nothing to fear except for being unkind, dishonest, etc.
We fear not being the best person we could be.
You can also check out Chapter 2.2 for some public speaking tips.
But sometimes, we could be proactive in the wrong place.
So the next chapter answers:
“How do you know that?”
Because then, if we know we are doing something wrong, we can be proactive and change ourselves.

title: 4.2 Building and Breaking Habits
section: 4. Procrastinating? Learn to put the first things first.
content:
4.2 Building and Breaking Habits# (Note)
This section is based on what I learned from Atomic Habits.
I see this section as a generalized version of Chapter 4.1. I wrote about dealing with scrolling first because I thought it would be more useful.
Anyways…
TLDR# The repetition of behavior, not the sincerity of belief, is what shapes character.
You become the kind of person who does the things you repeatedly do.
Habits have a loop:
Cue Craving Response or routine Reward If you want to make a habit, make it:
Obvious Attractive Easy Satisfying If you want to break a habit, make it:
Invisible Unattractive Difficult Unsatisfying You can cut the loop anywhere, and then you cannot execute the habit as easily.
Bad habits repeat themselves not because you do not want to change, but because you have the wrong system for change.
You do not rise to the level of your goals.
You fall to the level of your systems.
Small changes often appear to make no difference until you cross a critical threshold.
The most powerful outcomes of any compounding process are delayed.
You need to be patient.
Context Matters More Than We Think# We do not like to think this, but a lot of our habits depend on our context.
If all our friends are chilling, we will almost always feel at least some urge to go chill too.
If we are in a library where everybody is locked in, like during AP test season, we will almost always feel the urge to lock in too.
What matters is that you stick with something.
Make sure you actually get reps in before you start optimizing.
The Atomic Habits Rules# The habit loop is:
Cue → Craving → Response/Routine → Reward
To make a habit, make it:
Obvious Attractive Easy Satisfying To break a habit, make it:
Invisible Unattractive Difficult Unsatisfying Example: Dealing With Justin’s YouTube Bingeing# First, let’s assess why I had a habit of bingeing YouTube.
What was actually going on?
As we know from above, every habit is formed by its loop:
Cue Craving Response/Routine Reward Let’s examine each one.
Cue# Watching YouTube was very obvious to me because I was constantly using the internet.
Also, struggling with difficult studies is uncomfortable.
That led to a feeling like:
“I would do anything to stave off my suffering.”
So when I opened a browser to study, YouTube was already nearby.
Craving# Since I was uncomfortable, I wanted to get rid of that discomfort.
So I would look around for quick relief.
Response# Ah!
YouTube was just a few buttons away:
Command + T → y → YouTube autofills → Enter
By that point, my fingers could do the full motion in a snap.
Reward# Watching YouTube felt satisfying.
It was satisfying to see a new cat video, a street chef cooking, pro gamers smoking their competitors, or new speedrun tech.
Then the thought became:
“Oh man, let’s keep watching!”
Then I would click on random Minecraft videos.
Why This Became a Habit# This became a habit because it was:
Obvious Attractive Easy Satisfying It was super obvious.
I watched YouTube a lot, so every time I opened a browser to study, I knew there was a very high chance I would end up bingeing.
It was attractive, especially when I was grappling with hard concepts from class.
It was incredibly easy to lose sight of my goals once I started watching videos.
I also genuinely like watching professional athletes and mathletes do their craft.
It was absolutely easy.
It was literally four buttons away.
But the weird part is that it was not actually that satisfying.
It was just a little more satisfying than studying, which was enough to get me started.
Then I would feel horrible afterward.
That created a downward spiral:
Discomfort → Video → Regret → More discomfort → Another video
Yeah, this did not go well for me.
So How Did I Break This?# Remember the rules from above.
If we want to break a habit, we make it:
Invisible Unattractive Difficult Unsatisfying I really tried to get rid of this habit, so I attacked all four stages of the loop.
My solution is not perfect, but it still helps me.
Hopefully, it can help you too.
See Chapter 4.1 for more details.
Make It Invisible# To make YouTube invisible, I minimized the chances that I would be alone with my device.
I kept myself busy.
When I made time for solitude, I asked my mom or friends to hide my device until I got my work done.
This reduced the number of obvious cues around me.
Make It Unattractive# This was the biggest part of the solution.
It was also the first part I tackled.
I had to convince myself that watching YouTube was bad for me, even though it could feel like colorful hypnosis.
I convinced myself that bingeing videos made my problems worse.
I also realized I would not want to be remembered for bingeing videos.
That made the habit less attractive.
Make It Difficult# This part was hard.
I installed extensions that blocked recommendations.
I turned off my watch history so I would not get jumped by a horde of wonderful videos screaming for my attention whenever I opened YouTube.
On a smaller note, I removed the apps too.
The goal was to add friction.
Make It Unsatisfying# To make YouTube unsatisfying, I maximized the chances that I could get caught.
For me, that meant going to a professional environment like SF State’s library or a teacher’s office.
If I got caught by a teacher, friend, or even a smart-looking student, I would feel at least a little queasy.
I was not really “getting caught.”
It was more like they would shoot me an:
“Are you serious?”
glance.
And that tanked my ego.
So YouTube did not feel as great anymore.
The Biggest Lesson# The biggest thing I learned from dealing with my bad YouTube habit was this:
I should not have done it all by myself.
If I had asked others how they dealt with this, and if I had consulted books earlier, I probably would have found this solution, or an even better one, in half the time.
Remember, you are not alone.
Leverage the people around you.
Even if they are not geniuses, they can grow with you.
That makes it more fun too.
Atomic Habits Practices# I think it is okay to end the chapter here.
But if you would like some nice practices from Atomic Habits, here you go.
I highly recommend the book, by the way.
The 1st Law: Make It Obvious# 1.1 Fill Out the Habits Scorecard# Write down your current habits to become aware of them.
1.2 Use Implementation Intentions# Use this format:
“I will [BEHAVIOR] at [TIME] in [LOCATION].”
1.3 Use Habit Stacking# Use this format:
“After [CURRENT HABIT], I will [NEW HABIT].”
1.4 Design Your Environment# Make the cues of good habits obvious and visible.
Inversion of the 1st Law: Make It Invisible# 1.5 Reduce Exposure# Remove the cues of your bad habits from your environment.
The 2nd Law: Make It Attractive# 2.1 Use Temptation Bundling# Pair an action you want to do with an action you need to do.
2.2 Join a Culture Where Your Desired Behavior Is Normal# Join a group where the behavior you want is already the normal behavior.
2.3 Create a Motivation Ritual# Do something you enjoy immediately before a difficult habit.
Inversion of the 2nd Law: Make It Unattractive# 2.4 Reframe Your Mindset# Highlight the benefits of avoiding your bad habits.
Ask yourself what kind of person you want to become.
One possible exercise:
Imagine your funeral. What do you want people to remember you for?
The 3rd Law: Make It Easy# 3.1 Reduce Friction# Decrease the number of steps between you and your good habits.
3.2 Prime the Environment# Prepare your environment to make future actions easier.
3.3 Master the Decisive Moment# Optimize the small choices that deliver outsized impact.
3.4 Use the Two-Minute Rule# Downscale your habits until they can be done in two minutes or less.
3.5 Automate Your Habits# Invest in technology and one-time purchases that lock in future behavior.
Inversion of the 3rd Law: Make It Difficult# 3.6 Increase Friction# Increase the number of steps between you and your bad habits.
3.7 Use a Commitment Device# Restrict your future choices to the ones that benefit you.
The 4th Law: Make It Satisfying# 4.1 Use Reinforcement# Give yourself an immediate reward when you complete your habit.
4.2 Make “Doing Nothing” Enjoyable# When avoiding a bad habit, design a way to see the benefits.
4.3 Use a Habit Tracker# Keep track of your habit streak.
Do not break the chain.
4.4 Never Miss Twice# When you forget to do a habit, make sure you get back on track immediately.
Inversion of the 4th Law: Make It Unsatisfying# 4.5 Get an Accountability Partner# Ask someone to watch your behavior.
4.6 Create a Habit Contract# Make the costs of your bad habits public and painful.
Final Reminder# Habits are not only about motivation.
They are about systems.
If you want better habits, design a better system.
Make good habits obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
Make bad habits invisible, unattractive, difficult, and unsatisfying.
Then get your reps in.

title: 3.0 Finding Your Way? Listen, Then Experiment!
content:
3.0 Finding Your Way? Listen, Then Experiment!# TLDR# “Learn the rules like a pro, so you can break them like an artist.”
You are not wise enough to ignore everyone else. Don’t lean only on your own understanding.
Talking to others, listening carefully, reading books, and experimenting are some of the fastest ways to improve. Human beings are social creatures, and we adapt to our surroundings, so choose the best crowd and environment you can.
Spend most of your time learning from others, but still leave some time for solitude and original thinking. Personally, I like an 80/20 split: around 80% learning from others and 20% trying my own ideas.
Challenge yourself just enough. Like a rubber band, if you stretch too far, you snap; if you do not stretch enough, you do not grow.
When you are unsure how high to aim, err on the side of too hard.
1. Your Problems Are Probably Not That Rare# It took me a while to realize that many of my problems were not as unique as I thought.
For example, I tried to “deep think” my way out of my YouTube addiction from 9th to 11th grade. I spent years trying to solve the problem alone, thinking I was the only person who was “solving” a great problem of our age.
Then my brother told me he had basically been handed a better version of my solution by his college professor.
Feels bad, man.
That experience taught me something important: sometimes, someone else has already solved a cleaner version of your problem.
You do not always need to suffer for years just to rediscover what someone else could have explained to you in ten minutes.
I tried to distill my own solution, my brother’s solution, and ideas from books into a better system for dealing with scrolling.
The lesson is not “never think for yourself.”
The lesson is:
Think for yourself, but you don’t need to rediscover fire when people are driving cars.
Don’t trick yourself into trying to solo everything.
2. Reading Is Compressed Lifeforce# Why is reading so powerful?
Because authors have spent their lifeforce turning their best thoughts into something digestible for you.
Why do I love books?
A good book is years, sometimes decades, and sometimes lifetimes, of people’s experience, mistakes, thinking, research, and suffering compressed into a form you can absorb.
A bogus response is:
“But I won’t understand unless I try for myself!”
Part of the art of writing is taking great ideas and making them accessible for you to understand.
So it is foolish to think you can easily outsmart wise people by refusing to read them.
Again, from the previous point, you don’t need to rediscover fire in the age of cars.
Trying to “solo think” the early stages of discovery often just becomes a horribly inefficient way to reach the same understanding as someone who simply read the book, listened to the teacher, or followed good instructions.
If you are rebellious, challenge the teacher after.
One of my favorite quotes summarizes this:
“Learn the rules like a pro, so you can break them like an artist.”
3. Another Reason Not to Solo Before Listening# I made this mistake most obviously in learning math.
I was stubborn and afraid to ask questions. I convinced myself that if I could self-discover as much math as possible, then I would become the Lowell math genius.
People would praise me for my intellect, and I could goof off in class while getting straight A’s.
That did not work.
Instead, I kept banging my head against a wall that I refused to recognize.
I would tell myself:
“Just one more bang. Just put in one more hour tonight and you’ll become an Olympian.”
But that was not grit.
That was inefficient pride.
There is a difference between productive struggle and refusing to use help.
A better approach is:
Cover your bases first. Then try to discover.
Read the textbook. Listen to the teacher. Ask questions. Learn the standard methods.
Then, once you have the tools, go struggle and use your grit.
4. Some Warnings# Change your worldview, but do not become someone who only consumes information and never acts.
As discussed earlier in Chapter 1.2, do not overlearn without acting. That is effectively amnesia.
It is usually not worth spending an entire day falling behind in a hard class just because you are trying to rediscover every single idea on your own.
Instead, put the material into your head from the book, teacher, or example, then use it.
Spend more time sweating than watching people sweat.
If you selected a good teacher, class, or book, the chance to truly lock in and discover something will come naturally.
And when it does, it feels amazing to solve a problem after a week of attempts.
5. Choose Your Environment Carefully# We are social creatures.
We adapt to our surroundings.
That means your environment matters a lot.
If you are constantly around people who are lazy, cynical, distracted, or unserious, you will probably absorb some of that.
If you are around people who read, train, ask questions, build things, and take life seriously, you will probably absorb some of that too.
So surround yourself with the best environment and crowd you can find.
Then spend lots of time learning what that crowd is doing.
Watch their habits. Listen to how they talk. Ask how they think. Notice what they prioritize.
But also spend some time alone every day.
Solitude matters because it gives you space to digest what you are learning and decide what you actually believe.
My personal split is roughly:
80% learning from others, 20% thinking and experimenting on my own.
6. Challenge Yourself Like a Rubber Band# Growth requires tension.
Think of a rubber band.
If you stretch it too little, nothing happens.
If you stretch it too far, it breaks.
Your challenge level should work the same way.
Too easy, and you stagnate.
Too hard, and you burn out or shut down.
Just hard enough, and you grow.
So how high should you aim?
When unsure, err on the side of too hard.
Not insanely hard.
Not self-destructive.
But slightly beyond what feels comfortable.
Remember that you have to move beyond your comfort zone.
7. Experiment Instead of Endlessly Pondering# As mentioned earlier in Chapter 1.2, action is one of the best ways to get new maps.
You do not always know what works by thinking.
Sometimes you have to test it.
Try a new study method.
Try waking up earlier.
Try asking questions in class.
Hell, even try sleeping in class.
Try talking to that random person on the bus who looks interesting.
Remember, you can’t really fail, because no one really cares.
The duration can vary.
Elisa might test something for a week.
I might test something for at least three days.
The exact number is not the main point.
The main point is:
Actually test things.
If you experiment, you are collecting information from reality, not just from your imagination.
Life is smarter than your ruminations.
Final Reminder# You do not need to figure everything out alone.
Learn from others.
Read books.
Ask questions.
Choose your environment carefully.
Challenge yourself.
Experiment.
Then reflect.
Don’t have blind obedience, but don’t spam arrogant solo thinking either.
Remember, we don’t have all the answers.
Sometimes you just have to do things, get your hands dirty, and find out.

title: 4. Procrastinating? Learn to put the first things first.
content:
4.0 Procrastinating? Put First Things First# TLDR# Being able to put first things first is a major requirement if you want to get good work done.
We can make it easier.
This chapter pulls from ideas in The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People, Deep Work, and Digital Minimalism.
The Problem# Some days, we have something important to us.
So we might tell ourselves:
“I’m going to get all my homework done!”
But then the distractions come in during our focused time.
Maybe we want to watch that new YouTube video.
Maybe our friends sit next to us and start scrolling.
Maybe we start multitasking and pretend we are still working.
We are not advocating for an anti-fun policy.
Who likes that?
We are not saying you need to live like a hermit either.
But when we need to get something done, we should avoid distracting ourselves as much as possible until the work is finished.
The goal is simple:
Do not procrastinate. Avoid distractions. Do the important thing first.
What Are Some Good Methods?# Focus on prioritizing tasks throughout the day instead of relying only on rigid time schedules.
Know that you cannot have everything at once.
Multiple times per day, you may need to weigh the benefits of focused work against the temptation of getting distracted.
We find it easier to plan by setting priorities each day.
But we also have to admit that we cannot have everything at the same time.
Plan out what important things need to be done.
For example:
“Write college applications at the library after school.”
Then focus on one thing at a time.
No multitasking.
I feel like multitasking is a horrible way to work.
A Simple Example# Locking in for homework during class time, then talking with friends afterward, is better than half-doing homework while yapping with friends.
To be honest, half-doing homework while talking with friends still looks really nice.
But I know my grade probably will not.
Willpower Alone Is Not Enough# Relying on willpower alone does not work consistently.
If you tell yourself:
“Oh, I just need a little bit more discipline, then I will get it done.”
Then be prepared to face the mirror at night and realize the whole day passed without anything getting done.
I feel you.
That has happened an embarrassingly high number of times to me.
Our daily willpower varies, and it is limited.
Some days we feel like going full sicko mode.
Other days, our willpower goes poof.
So is there a better way?
Yes.
Use your willpower for things that actually matter.
Do not burn it all fighting distractions.
Avoid the Battle Before It Starts# If we are constantly battling to focus, our easier habits will eventually win over the harder but more beneficial habits.
It is not worth using a bunch of willpower to fight off distractions that could have been avoided beforehand.
So how can we avoid unnecessary distractions before we get tempted?
1. Make Your Priorities Clear# Make it clear what your priorities are.
Clarifying why something is important to you helps too.
A big yes to what is important helps you say no to things that are ultimately trivial.
Ask yourself:
“What actually matters right now?”
Then act based on that answer.
2. Go to a Better Environment# Automate your focus by going to a good environment.
For example, lock in during class or work in a library.
When your environment supports the thing you want to do, you do not need to fight as hard.
A good environment makes the right action easier.
A bad environment makes the wrong action easier.
3. Focus on One Thing at a Time# Focus on only one thing at a time.
Do not try to do homework, text people, watch videos, and think deeply at the same time.
That is usually fake productivity.
If you need to focus, remove the distractions before you start.
Throw your phone out the window before you get to work.
Okay, maybe do not actually throw it out the window.
But you get the point.
4. Ask Yourself the Hard Question# An important practice I use when facing procrastination is asking myself:
“Why would I choose to put my most important task at the mercy of something less important?”
And also:
“If this really was important to me, why would I choose to hurt myself?”
That question hurts a little.
But it works.
It reminds me that procrastination is not just “being lazy.”
Sometimes it is choosing something less important over something I actually care about.
Environment Steers the Odds# When you say yes to something that is truly important to you, and when you get into an environment where it is easier to do that important thing, it will not completely prevent procrastination.
You can still get distracted.
You can still mess up.
But it will steer the odds in your favor.
And sometimes that is enough.
Making a Plan# I do not have a definite formula.
Do what works for you.
But do something.
If it helps, use me as an example.
This is me thinking out loud as I plan.
Example: Me Planning Out Loud# It is 10:30 p.m. on 4/12/26.
Wow, all evens.
As an example:
I stopped writing for about a week because of college apps.
I am procrastinating.
I am also procrastinating on setting up my math tournament and a mentor program at middle school.
Why?
In short, I am busy.
I have a lot of things to do.
But what are those things?
More importantly, what is stopping me from moving forward?
What Is Actually Stopping Me?# I have to compete in a math tournament this Saturday.
That happens to be the same day as Prom, which might complicate things.
This is also my first time directing our wonderful Math-ardinals for this occasion, so I better not screw up.
So, yeah.
I am busy.
I also want to get my math club’s new board established.
Only then can I proceed.
But I do not think it is good to keep the principal hanging, especially because I also got my old math teachers to join in.
So the next move should be obvious.
What Can I Do Right Now?# I can send them an email follow-up.
It is going to look something like this:
Yo wassup! Yeah, I currently have a lot on my plate.
Okay, maybe I should make it more professional than that.
But the point is that I can do something now.
Real Reasons vs. Fake Excuses# Upon further inspection, I notice I have some fake excuses.
This is a good example.
Fake Excuse 1# I am arranging a guest speaker for math club, but he has not gotten back to me.
This is a fake excuse because I do not need to worry about it until he responds.
There is nothing for me to do there right now.
Fake Excuse 2# Another math tournament: Purple Comet.
This is also fake because I have done it since freshman year.
So why should I worry about it that much if I already know what will happen at least 70% of the time?
Side Note: Purple Comet# If you are reading this, why not join in the fun?
Math is fun, and it is not that disappointing.
Especially when you get cool friends to be your companions in the two-plus-hour struggle for your sanity.
A Useful Anti-Procrastination Exercise# This exercise has helped me a lot.
Ask yourself:
What have I been procrastinating on?
Why?
What is stopping me now or later?
What are the real reasons?
What are the fake reasons?
What is something I can do right now?
I am trying to constantly improve this for the future.
Final Reminder# You do not beat procrastination by only yelling at yourself to have more discipline.
You beat it by making the important thing clear.
You beat it by choosing a better environment.
You beat it by focusing on one thing at a time.
You beat it by noticing your fake excuses.
And most importantly:
You beat it by doing something now.

title: 5.0 Struggling or Getting Stuck?
content:
5.0 Struggling or Getting Stuck?# Study Tips: Cover Your Bases, Ask Questions, and Keep Going# TLDR# Cover your bases.
Do your homework, sleep well, and ask questions in class.
“If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants.”
— Isaac Newton
Focus is good because it helps you get quality practice, but do not try to solo everything.
This environment does not reward soloing as much as drawing knowledge from others.
Problem solving requires patience, perseverance, and accepting that being stuck is part of the process.
Be consistent with the simple things.
Do not overthink them so much that you end up not doing them.
The Problem# Some classes are hard.
Some are boring.
Some are straight-up mental acrobatics.
And sometimes, we run into the classic:
“I am well cooked.”
scenario.
We have an essay due tomorrow, and we have not started.
Oh, we also have a math test tomorrow.
And this physics equation looks like some medley of Greek letters.
Oh, and there is also a horde of finals right before AP exams.
Me walking into class knowing Avogadro is a fruit…
In short:
We get overwhelmed.
Why This Happens# Let’s take a step back and look at the causes of this distressingly common scenario.
Some common causes are:
Wrong view of creativity Procrastination and distraction Fear of struggle Cramming and all-nighters Here are some solutions:
Put in consistent effort and learn to move on. Struggle just enough. Choose a good environment. Prioritize. Instead of Trying to Be Creative, Put In Consistent Effort# You will get inspired and have those creative “aha!” moments if you are patient and put in consistent effort.
Do not get stuck “waiting for brilliance.”
Our school environment has wonderful resources, like teachers, friends, and even that random dude in the corner.
Use them.
This is especially true when we take hard classes.
Trying to solo everything drains so much energy, so use other people’s ideas and feedback to learn in addition to your focused practice.
Author Tiago Forte says this well:
“The myth of a writer sitting down before a blank page, or the artist on a completely blank canvas, is just that—a myth. Professional creatives constantly draw on outside sources of inspiration: their own experiences and observations, lessons gleaned from successes and failures alike, and the ideas of others. If there is a secret to creativity, it is that it emerges from everyday efforts to gather and organize our thoughts and ideas.”
Newton is known for his genius work, and yet he also admits that he did not do everything alone:
“If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants.”
This does not mean you should neglect your solo time.
Draw a balance.
Afraid of Struggling? Struggle Enough, Then Learn to Move On# For most of us, it is common to despair when we struggle with hard problems.
Fear not.
Struggling is not failing.
Struggling is part of learning.
It is not proof that we are bad at something.
In math classes, it is common to struggle a lot.
Andrew Wiles, winner of a Fields Medal, says that struggle can feel like failure, but it is not.
Patience, perseverance, and acceptance define a mathematician.
So:
Problem solving = patience, perseverance, and accepting that being stuck is part of the process.
We should challenge ourselves, but we should not overwhelm ourselves.
Too little challenge, and we stagnate.
Too much challenge, and we panic or burn out.
Struggling is part of learning, and so is learning to move on.
When we spend too long on a problem to the point that we fall behind, it can be smart to cut our losses and move on.
We will have more problems to do.
Sometimes, it is okay to give up, learn the solution, and then try again later after you have caught up with class.
Remember, we get better over time.
It is not worth rabbit-holing on a single problem in exchange for your grades.
Prelearning# Prelearning is a great tactic, especially in harder classes.
It basically means this:
Before your class goes over something, go read up on it.
Do not rabbit-hole too hard if you do not understand something.
Instead, write down what you do not understand and ask the teacher when the moment comes.
This way, class becomes less like hearing a foreign language for the first time and more like filling in gaps.
Problem-Solving Tip: Start Simple Before Going Complex# Start with the simple before the complex.
Isn’t it way more efficient to get the kill with a one-damage punch rather than using:
Collapsing Star Roaring Cannon Mega Ultra…
plus ten more intimidating adjectives?
We have limited time.
Only do the ultra-complicated move if this is the last problem you will ever do.
You get it.
Solving a problem is like checking what tools you actually have before sprinting down Complexity Road toward Genius Gymnasium on Delusion Drive.
Cramming? Prioritize the Big Rocks First# Cramming happens when we force effort into a short amount of time, even though that effort should have been spread out over a longer period.
To deal with this, consider this question:
Have you ever tried fitting big rocks and small rocks into a container?
If you put the small rocks in first and then the big rocks, the small rocks use up a lot of space.
But if you put the big rocks in first and then the small rocks, the small rocks fit nicely between the big rocks.
This uses the space much better.
Fitting rocks is similar to managing our daily tasks.
A lot of the time, we end up cramming our big rocks at the end because the small rocks have eaten up the day.
We should treat our important tasks like big rocks and less important tasks like small rocks.
Big rocks before small rocks.
That way, we do not need to cram as much.
Also:
Cramming and pulling all-nighters do not work well.
Procrastination and Distraction? Prioritize Environment Over Willpower# You might say:
“But I can’t help but have my small rocks eat up my time! I don’t have that discipline!”
That is why we should prioritize having a great environment over relying on constant self-discipline.
A good environment favors the things you want to do.
If you want to party, go somewhere like a beach.
If you want to focus, go to a library.
Though studying at a beach does not sound too bad…
A well-chosen environment can even make boring things seem fun.
On the other hand, a bad environment quietly destroys your effort.
It wastes your time and energy.
If you are trying to study next to people who are laughing their heads off at reels, you will probably be disappointed when you do not get any work done.
You will also feel drained from trying to ignore their screaming.
A good environment is like an infinite money glitch.
So please, do not lie to yourself when you pretend you are focused in a horrible environment.
A great application of this is when you consider heading home to work.
Experiment with this:
Do not go straight home if home kills your focus.
It is common for us to see the bed or computer and have our motivation crash out.
Stay realistic.
Work where you work well.
Digital Environments Matter Too# For digital environments, it helps to avoid temptation rather than confront it.
Personally, I block recommendation feeds and move to a quiet classroom to get my work done.
When I do that, I do not feel as much need to distract myself.
If you need more, check out Chapter 4 on procrastination.
Let Teachers Help You# Lastly, choose to let teachers help you.
A good teacher saves you time.
They can offer valuable feedback and point you in the right direction so you do not wander around in confusion while trying to understand the basic material.
It is hard to grind alone and decode material by yourself.
It is supposed to be like that.
That is why we have teachers.
Final Summary# School gets overwhelming when we procrastinate, isolate ourselves, fear struggle, and mismanage priorities.
But with a good environment, consistent effort, smart prioritization, and the right attitude toward being stuck, learning becomes much more manageable.
It can even become fun.

title: 6.0 Tired? Rest Properly
content:
6.0 Tired? Rest Properly# TLDR# Sleep is not optional.
Sleep is goated.
Rest does not mean bingeing on your device.
Real breaks should help us recover, not trap us.
Sleep Either Helps You or Breaks You# Why We Sleep is a 368-page book with one very clear message:
Sleep or suffer.
The book uses evidence from science to show how powerful sleep is.
Good sleep helps memory, learning, mood, focus, and health.
Bad sleep hurts all of those.
You will not do well in school if you do not sleep.
It is usually not worth pulling an all-nighter just to study more or finish more homework.
Cramming works for some people.
It sometimes works for most of us.
But be honest with yourself:
If your cramming system leaves you tired, stressed, inconsistent, and unable to sustain your work, then it probably is not the way to move forward.
It is recommended to sleep at least eight hours, though you can adjust based on yourself.
Some people may say:
“Oh, my body will adjust.”
That might feel true, but getting used to bad sleep does not mean bad sleep is okay.
For more reasons why we should sleep, you can also read Walker’s book Why We Sleep.
Experiment With Waking Up Early# Experiment with whether waking up early fits you well.
Taking a Block 0 may or may not help you.
If you do well waking up early, it could be useful.
But it could also cut into your natural sleep, especially because adolescents’ natural sleep schedules tend to shift later.
If it does not hurt your sleep, waking up earlier can help you focus more easily.
The morning is very quiet.
Also cold.
But the quiet can make it much easier to focus with fewer distractions.
Still:
Do not destroy your sleep.
Okay, you probably get the point.
We should sleep properly.
But what about resting during the day?
Using Entertainment? Do Not Binge Brain Rot# Do not binge YouTube, TikTok, or other platforms to “rest.”
Go hang out with friends.
Boredom can also be a nice way to rest.
A fun and positive crowd can make life much better.
For example, choir can be a really fun crowd.
I used to mistake that kind of group as lazy.
But looking back, it was actually goated.
I wish I had stayed instead of bingeing YouTube videos alone.
The YouTube Break Trap# When I used YouTube to take breaks, I was basically walking into a trap.
Here is what usually happened to me:
“I’ll watch one video.”
Ten minutes pass.
Beep.
“Okay, break ended—oh, that video looks really good!”
Then 20 minutes pass.
Then 40 minutes pass.
Then I am tired.
Worse, I feel guilty because I just messed up my focus.
If you really want to use entertainment, make sure it brings you closer to people you care about.
Do not get trapped when you use it.
When planning how to take a break, ask yourself:
“Will this help me come back stronger, or make it harder to return?”
Again:
Breaks should not become indulgence.
But What If I Already Doomscrolled?# A lot of people have probably had an experience like mine above.
We lose control and scroll.
Then we suddenly realize we should have been grinding.
It is like the moment your mom opens the door and you get jumpscared harder than when playing Five Nights at Freddy’s.
Here is what I do:
For the next few minutes, usually around five minutes, I do something easy.
For example, I get up and organize my things.
This helps me restart without needing a huge amount of willpower.
The goal is not to punish yourself.
The goal is to gently re-enter motion.
Experiment: Try Out Boredom# Sometimes, getting some time to be bored helps us recover.
Experiment with yourself.
But make sure boredom does not turn into brain rotting.
This is personal, so test it.
Be honest about whether your “rest” is actually restoring you or just numbing you.
Try out different suggestions and notice what actually helps.
Make Breaks Social When You Can# I went through a phase where I tried to make even my breaks “efficient.”
I practiced pen spinning, juggling, mental math, and working out during breaks because I wanted to be the type of person who could do hard math problems and cool tricks.
The issue was that I was alone for too long.
Studying alone in a secluded place, then taking a break alone to practice some skill, was mentally taxing.
By the time I had to return to work, I often felt even more frustrated.
Earlier, I said we can experiment with being bored.
The reason I add “make sure you do not end up brain rotting” is because when we are tired after a focused session, it is hard to resist temptation.
This is especially true when we are alone.
So I suggest:
Make breaks social when possible.
Go laugh with friends who were also locked in.
Other people can steer our behavior for better or for worse.
Try not to join the people who are brain rotting if you still need to get work done.
You might get pulled in too.
You probably will, because we are human.
Prepare Your Environment Before You Work# Set up your future self for success.
Your future self will probably be tired.
And when you are tired, you are easier to distract.
That means you are also more likely to binge.
This is where a pre-mortem helps.
A post-mortem asks:
“What went wrong?”
after something fails.
A pre-mortem asks:
“What might go wrong?”
before it happens.
Before committing to a focused session, I ask myself:
“What in my environment will help me or distract me?”
Then I remove the possible problems before they become obstacles.
Examples:
Put my phone away. Give my phone to my friends. Clean unnecessary things off my desk. Move to the best spot I can find. Prepare my materials. Refill my lead. There is a quote that sums this up:
“Victorious warriors win first, then go fight the battle.”
Our battle of focus is easier when our environment helps us.
Good Rules for Rest# Sleep well.
Make breaks social when possible.
Avoid breaks that turn into addiction.
Prepare your environment before working.
If you mess up and doomscroll, rebuild momentum with something easy.
Some Suggestions# Try these when you need a real break:
Walk around. Talk or laugh with friends. Journal. Clean your space. Run errands. Sit quietly for a few minutes. Final Reminder# Rest is supposed to help you recover.
It should make it easier to return to your life, your work, and your people.
Sleep properly.
Take breaks that actually restore you.
Do not call something “rest” if it leaves you more trapped, tired, and guilty than before.
